turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has our opinion this morning in case 17-1594, Return Mail, Inc. v. USPS.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: The America Invents Act of 2011, hereinafter the AIA, established three types of administrative review proceedings that enable a person other than a patent owner to go before the Patent Trial and Appeal Board and challenge the validity of an existing patent. After hearing a person's challenge to a patent, the Board either confirms the patent claims or cancel some or all of them. Any dissatisfied party may seek judicial review of the Board's decision. The question presented in this case is whether a federal agency is a person eligible to petition for these AIA review proceedings? The answer matters in this case because Return Mail, the petitioner, had its patent canceled by the Board after the United States Postal Service initiated an AIA proceeding in response to an infringement suit against the Postal Service. If the government is not a person eligible to bring such a challenge, then it should not have been able to contest Return Mail's patent in the AIA proceeding. We hold today that the government is not a person capable of instituting the three AIA review proceedings. In reaching this conclusion, we apply the long-standing interpretive presumption that the word 'person' in a statute excludes federal agencies like the Postal Service. To overcome this presumption, the Postal Service must point to some indication in the AIA's textual context that affirmatively shows Congress intended to include the government as a person. Although, the Postal Service makes several arguments in an attempt to make that showing, none delivers. The Postal Service first argues -- takes a while, right? The Postal Service first argues that the AIA's reference to a person in this context must include the government because other references to persons in the patent statutes appear to do so. The Postal Service invokes the principal that when Congress uses a word to mean one thing in one part of the statute, it usually means the same thing elsewhere in the statute. But sometimes, a statutory term takes on different meanings throughout the statute; such is the case here. There are at least 18 references to persons throughout the Patent Act and the AIA, some of which plainly include or exclude the government and others that might be read either way. Accordingly, the mere existence of some government-inclusive references does not overcome the presumption. Next, the Postal Service points to the federal government's long-standing history of applying for patents but the government's ability to obtain a patent does not speak to whether Congressman for the government to participate as a third-party challenger in AIA proceedings created only eight years ago, nor are we persuaded by the patent office's view that the government is among those eligible to request a so-called ex parte re-examination, which is yet another type of re-examination process available for an issued patent. That process is wholly internal to the patent office and is fundamentally different from an adversarial adjudicatory review proceeding under the AIA. Thus, Congress may well have had good reasons to permit the government to initiate one but not the other. Finally, the Postal Service contends that it would be anomalous to deny it a benefit afforded to other infringers, namely the ability to challenge a patent de novo before the patent officer. We see no (inaudible) though. In Congress affording non-governmental actors, an expedient route to challenge patents that federal agencies do not also enjoy. In this context, non-governmental actors face greater and more uncertain risks if they misjudge their right to use patented technology. Most notably, a patent owner who sues the government may recover only reasonable and entire compensation for the infringing use; whereas a patent owner in an infringement suit against a non-governmental actor may seek an injunction, demand a jury trial or ask for punitive damages. We are therefore un-persuaded that the government's exclusion from the AIA review proceedings is sufficiently anomalous to overcome the presumption that the government is not a person under the Act. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit and remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Breyer has filed a dissenting opinion in which Justice Ginsburg and Justice Kagan have joined.
